title: Inflation Is a Memory
synopsis: Prices are rising. The CPI ticked up this quarter in ways that made economists uncomfortable, though the discomfort was expressed in the careful la...
preview: Prices are rising. The CPI ticked up this quarter in ways that made economists uncomfortable, though the discomfort was expressed in the careful la...
content:
Prices are rising. The CPI ticked up this quarter in ways that made economists uncomfortable, though the discomfort was expressed in the careful language that economists use when they are worried but not yet willing to say so. Transitory, they said. Supply chain disruptions. Base effects. Temporary pressures that will resolve as the pandemic recedes and normality returns.

Transitory. I want to remember this word, because I suspect it will define this era's monetary misjudgment the way irrational exuberance defined the last one.

Here is what I observe. Energy costs are rising, and the supply constraints that produce rising energy costs are not temporary. They are structural: underinvestment in production, regulatory barriers to new capacity, the geopolitical reshuffling that is making energy into a strategic weapon. Food costs are rising, driven by the same energy costs that feed into every input of the agricultural system, fertiliser, transport, processing, cold storage. Shipping costs remain elevated because the container fleet was not built for a pandemic recovery and cannot be expanded on the timeline the recovery demands. Labour costs are rising because the pandemic reshuffled the workforce in ways that created shortages in sectors that cannot automate quickly enough to compensate.

None of these pressures are transitory. Each one reflects a structural shift that will take years, not months, to resolve. The people calling this transitory are not lying. They are using a framework calibrated for a world that no longer exists.

I find myself thinking about institutional memory, because I think the absence of it explains more than the models do.

The people currently running the Reserve Bank of Australia, the Federal Reserve, the Bank of England, the ECB, the people making the decisions about interest rates and money supply and the response to rising prices, are, overwhelmingly, people who built their careers during the great disinflation. From the early 1990s through 2020, inflation in the developed world was low, stable, and declining. An entire generation of central bankers, economists, treasury officials, and financial professionals came of age in a world where inflation was not a problem.

This is not a small thing. Professional judgment is shaped by experience, and when the experience is thirty years of falling inflation, the judgment tends toward complacency about the possibility of rising inflation. The models were trained on disinflationary data. The intuitions were formed in disinflationary conditions. The institutional culture was optimised for a world where the risk was deflation, not inflation, and where the appropriate response to any economic stress was to lower rates and print money.

These people are now confronting something their careers did not prepare them for. And institutions staffed by people who have never navigated a thing tend to respond to that thing poorly.

The distributional impact is what concerns me most. Inflation is often discussed as if it were a uniform phenomenon, prices rise, everyone pays more, the economy adjusts. This is misleading. Inflation is distributive. It takes from some and gives to others, and the pattern of taking and giving is not random.

Inflation helps debtors and hurts creditors, because debts are denominated in nominal terms. If you owe $500,000 on a mortgage and inflation erodes the real value of that debt, you benefit. The bank that lent you the money loses. This is why homeowners with large mortgages are, in a perverse way, beneficiaries of moderate inflation.

But the people who suffer are the ones at the bottom. People on fixed incomes whose payments do not adjust with prices. People who hold cash savings that lose purchasing power. People whose wages lag behind price increases because their bargaining position is weak. Renters, whose housing costs rise with the market while their incomes do not.

Inflation is a regressive tax. It falls hardest on the people least able to bear it and least equipped to hedge against it. The wealthy own assets, property, equities, commodities, that rise with inflation. The poor hold cash that declines. The gap widens.

The historical record on this is not ambiguous. Every period of significant inflation in the twentieth century was accompanied by a widening of inequality, a deterioration in social trust, and political instability. The Weimar hyperinflation is the extreme case, but the pattern holds at lower levels. When prices rise faster than wages, people feel, correctly, that the system is failing them. They look for explanations, and the explanations they find are not always accurate, but they are always powerful.

Hyperinflation is not the prediction. Catastrophe is not the prediction. What I predict is that the current inflation will prove less transitory than the institutions managing it believe, and that the response will be slower and less effective than it needs to be, because the people making decisions have never made them under these conditions, and because the institutional incentive is always to hope that the problem resolves itself rather than to act preemptively and risk being wrong.

From Adelaide, watching the prices at the supermarket tick upward week by week, the word that keeps occurring to me is not transitory. It is unfamiliar. The institutions tasked with responding are encountering something unfamiliar, and unfamiliarity produces hesitation, and hesitation, in the face of rising prices, produces exactly the delay that allows the rise to entrench itself.

Inflation is not a number. It is an experience. And the people making decisions about it have never had the experience. This gap between the decision-makers and the reality they are managing is, I think, the most important gap to watch.
